text: We present a case of a robotic trans-anal recto-urethral fistula repair. Our patient is a sixty-eight-year-old male who is morbidly obese at a hundred and thirty-five kilograms who had a Gleason eight prostate cancer treated with robotic prostatectomy without any prior radiotherapy. There was no immediate post-operative complication. Unfortunately, he represented three weeks after discharge with pneumaturia and pelvic sepsis requiring laparoscopic washout and a defunctioning loop colostomy.

A retrograde cystogram showed contrast extravasation from the urethra to the rectum, confirming a recto-urethral fistula. Three months later, after resolution of his pelvic sepsis, we perform a robotic transanal recto-urethral repair utilizing the DaVinci XI platform.
The following is his CT retrograde cystogram confirming contrast extravasation from the vesicle-urethral junction into the rectum.

The patient is positioned in lithotomy with the da Vinci XI surgical cart on the left side. The boom is extended over the abdomen, and three robotic arms are utilized: one for the camera and two for dissecting instruments. The gel point path platform is used with the robotic port configuration visible.

The surgeon uses bipolar forceps and monopolar scissors to dissect and excise the fistula tract. The anatomy of the fistula is confirmed by visualizing the indwelling catheter within the vesico-urethral compartment.
The surgeon completes the circumferential dissection of the fistula tract and develops a superiorly based full thickness flap, ensuring dissection in the correct plane to minimize bleeding.

The surgeon develops a flap approximately two centimeters superiorly to ensure tension-free closure of the rectal opening of the fistula. The closure is performed in layers, beginning with the vesico-urethral defect using a continuous three OV lock suture.
The surgeon distends the bladder with water to verify the seal, then proceeds to close the rectal defect using a continuous three-hole V-lock suture, ensuring a secure closure.
The surgeon deploys T-cell fibrin sealant in the rectal vesicle space, ensuring the area is adequately sealed following the closure of the rectal defect.

At the three-month follow-up, we repeated the CT retrograde cystogram, which confirmed resolution of the recto-urethral fistula. After the cystogram, the patient's loop colostomy was reversed. At six months follow-up, there was no evidence of recurrence.
